attributes.title: Intro to Git
attributes.description: Understand what Git is, why it exists, and how it helps developers work safely and collaboratively.

post.html:
What is Missing Semesters?

Missing Semesters is a workshop series focused on teaching practical tools and workflows that are rarely taught in traditional classes, but are essential for real-world software development, productivity, and collaboration.

These are the skills you usually learn late, by accident, or the hard way.

This workshop is here to change that.






Who am I?

As-salamu alaykum 👋
I am Shafayetul Huda Sadi 🌱

You can find me here:

  • GitHub
  • LinkedIn
  • Twitter
  • Facebook

Missing Semester website:
👉 missing-semester






The Goal

To teach you enough of Git and GitHub:

  • Not do this:
    • project_final.zip
    • project_final_v2.zip
    • project_final_v3_REAL_last.zip
    • project_vai_eitai_last_ar_partesina.zip
  • Participate in hackathons
  • Contribute to open-source projects
  • Work like professional developers
  • Not become a VibeCoder






Let’s Imagine Some Real Scenarios

  • You are participating in a hackathon with your friends:
    • You handle the frontend UI
    • Your friend works on the backend API
    • How do you share code?
    • Email a .zip file? Google Drive? Pendrive?
  • You want to showcase what you have built to your seniors, teachers, or recruiters. How do you do that?
  • You have spent weeks working on a project with thousands of lines of code, and suddenly:
    • Your OS crashes [Windows Blue Screen of Death]
    • Your laptop stops booting
    • Everything is gone

These are not rare problems.
Every developer faces them.

👉 Git exists to solve exactly these problems.






What is Git?






Git?

Git is a free and open-source, distributed version control system designed to track changes in source code and help developers collaborate safely and efficiently.

In simple terms, Git allows you to:

  • Track every change you make to your code
  • Go back to any previous version (commit)
  • See who changed what and when
  • Work with others without overwriting each other’s work
  • Feel safe while experimenting and making mistakes

Git is not magic. It is a safety system for your work.

Think of Git as a save-game system for your projects. Every commit is a checkpoint you can return to later.






Why Should You Use Git?

Git helps you because:

  • You can rollback broken changes instead of panicking
  • You can experiment freely without fear of losing progress
  • You can collaborate without copying folders like
    project_final_v3_REAL_last.zip

Git turns chaos into history.






Quick but Important Note

Git and GitHub are not the same thing.

  • Git is a tool (software) that runs on your computer
  • GitHub is a platform that hosts Git repositories online and adds collaboration features

You can use Git without GitHub.
GitHub simply makes Git more powerful and easier to collaborate.

We’ll understand this difference clearly in the next lessons.
post.markdown:
## What is Missing Semesters? **Missing Semesters** is a workshop series focused on teaching practical tools and workflows that are rarely taught in traditional classes, but are essential for real-world software development, productivity, and collaboration. These are the skills you usually learn **late**, **by accident**, or **the hard way**. This workshop is here to change that.




## Who am I? As-salamu alaykum 👋
I am **Shafayetul Huda Sadi** 🌱 You can find me here: - [GitHub][github] - [LinkedIn][linkedin] - [Twitter][twitter] - [Facebook][facebook]
Missing Semester website: 👉 [missing-semester][ms] [github]: https://github.com/shafayetsadi [linkedin]: https://linkedin.com/in/shafayetsadi [twitter]: https://twitter.com/shafayet_sadi [facebook]: https://www.facebook.com/shafayet.sadi.7 [ms]: https://missing-semesters-ruet.github.io/missing-semester/




## The Goal To teach you enough of Git and GitHub: - Not do this: - `project_final.zip` - `project_final_v2.zip` - `project_final_v3_REAL_last.zip` - `project_vai_eitai_last_ar_partesina.zip` - Participate in hackathons - Contribute to open-source projects - Work like professional developers - ~~Not become a **VibeCoder**~~




## Let’s Imagine Some Real Scenarios - You are participating in a hackathon with your friends: - You handle the frontend UI - Your friend works on the backend API - How do you share code? - Email a `.zip` file? Google Drive? Pendrive? - You want to **showcase what you have built** to your seniors, teachers, or recruiters. How do you do that? - You have spent weeks working on a project with thousands of lines of code, and suddenly: - Your OS crashes **\[Windows Blue Screen of Death]** - Your laptop stops booting - Everything is gone These are **not rare problems**. Every developer faces them. > 👉 **Git exists to solve exactly these problems.**




## What is Git? ![Git XKCD](https://imgs.xkcd.com/comics/git.png)




## Git? > **Git** is a **free and open-source, distributed version control system** designed to track changes in source code and help developers collaborate safely and efficiently. In simple terms, Git allows you to: - Track every change you make to your code - Go back to any previous version (commit) - See who changed what and when - Work with others without overwriting each other’s work - Feel safe while experimenting and making mistakes > Git is not magic. It is a **safety system for your work**. Think of Git as a save-game system for your projects. Every commit is a checkpoint you can return to later.




## Why Should You Use Git? Git helps you because: - You can **rollback** broken changes instead of panicking - You can **experiment freely** without fear of losing progress - You can **collaborate** without copying folders like `project_final_v3_REAL_last.zip` Git turns chaos into history.




## Quick but Important Note **Git and GitHub are not the same thing.** - **Git** is a tool (software) that runs on your computer - **GitHub** is a platform that hosts Git repositories online and adds collaboration features You can use Git **without** GitHub. GitHub simply makes Git more powerful and easier to collaborate. > We’ll understand this difference clearly in the next lessons.
